title: An Unintentional Wildlife Sanctuary
text:
In the novel When There Are Wolves Again by E.J. Swift, the Chernobyl disaster and its legacy is extrapolated to a near future where natural habitats are depleted and precarious.

This work of eco-fiction deftly explores issues of possible paths to a future where animals return to a nature depleted area. In the real world, a parallel version of this story has been unfolding as nature is thriving around former nuclear power plants.

This is especially evident at the former Chernobyl plant in Ukraine, where the absence of human activity has enabled wildlife to flourish despite continuing radiation, 40 years after the nuclear disaster there.

A 2,600 km² exclusion zone was established following the world's worst civilian nuclear accident at Chernobyl in 1986, which released a radioactive cloud across Europe and led to the evacuation of around 115,000 people from the surrounding area. Almost immediately, radiation poisoning killed 31 plant workers and firefighters.

It is 40 years since the Chernobyl disaster that led to the creation of the Chernobyl Exclusion Zone (CEZ). Since 1986, it has turned into a thriving, unintentional wildlife sanctuary and a vast rewilding laboratory. The CEZ prohibits people living there, commercial activities, natural resource extraction and public access. Now the area is home to flourishing populations of large mammals.

quizlet: a parallel version of this story
is extrapolated to a near future

questions.question: Why does the text mention the novel When There Are Wolves Again?
options: To introduce a fictional example that parallels real ecological recovery
To explain how nuclear reactors work
To describe the history of Ukrainian literature
To argue that fiction is more accurate than science
questions.explanation: The novel is used to introduce the idea of animals returning to depleted natural areas, which is then compared with real events around Chernobyl.

questions.question: What has enabled wildlife to flourish around the former Chernobyl plant?
options: The complete disappearance of radiation
The absence of human activity
The arrival of new farming methods
The construction of artificial habitats
questions.explanation: The text states that the absence of human activity has enabled wildlife to flourish despite continuing radiation.

questions.question: What was one immediate consequence of the Chernobyl accident?
options: The return of European bison
The creation of a tourist resort
The death of 31 plant workers and firefighters from radiation poisoning
The disappearance of all wildlife from Ukraine
questions.explanation: The text says that radiation poisoning killed 31 plant workers and firefighters almost immediately.

questions.question: What does the CEZ prohibit?
options: Only scientific research
Only animal migration
People living there, commercial activities, resource extraction and public access
Only hunting and fishing
questions.explanation: The passage says the CEZ prohibits people living there, commercial activities, natural resource extraction and public access.

questions.question: How is the Chernobyl Exclusion Zone described forty years after the disaster?
options: As a thriving unintentional wildlife sanctuary
As a fully restored human city
As an agricultural centre
As a completely lifeless radioactive desert
questions.explanation: The text describes the CEZ as a thriving, unintentional wildlife sanctuary and a vast rewilding laboratory.
